mappings.rationale: Directly covers the hominid fossil record, primate relationships, and classification of Hominidae including Australopithecus, Homo habilis, Homo erectus, and Homo sapiens.
Covers the evolutionary process broadly, providing the theoretical framework for understanding human evolutionary relationships and phylogenetic continuity.
Explores Darwin's theory of evolution by natural selection, the foundational mechanism underlying human evolutionary development.
Covers the discovery and interpretation of fossil remains, directly relevant to the hominid fossil record and paleontology of primates.
Addresses methods for discovering and interpreting early human remains, relevant to the discovery and recognition of the hominid fossil record.
Covers the period encompassing human evolution from early hominids through Homo sapiens, including Neanderthals and Cro-Magnons.
Provides understanding of the genetic basis of evolutionary change, relevant to distinguishing characteristics of Hominidae and primate phylogenetics.
Modern genomic methods have transformed understanding of human evolutionary relationships with other primates and among fossil hominid lineages.
Covers anthropological perspectives on distinguishing characteristics of humans, including behavioral adaptations that differentiate Hominidae from Pongidae.
Dental morphology is a key morphological characteristic for classifying hominid fossils and inferring diet and behavioral adaptations.
Provides the broad evolutionary context for the emergence of primates and hominids within the history of life on Earth.
Primates are mammals, and understanding mammalian evolution provides context for the natural history of primate life and hominid emergence.
Africa is the cradle of human evolution; the continent's history provides geographic and environmental context for hominid fossil sites and Homo sapiens origins.
Relevant to understanding the disappearance of earlier hominid species such as Neanderthals and the survival of Homo sapiens.
Sexual selection is an important evolutionary mechanism in understanding contrasting adaptations and morphological characteristics of Hominidae.
Addresses philosophical questions about species classification and evolutionary theory relevant to hominid taxonomy and the principle of phylogenetic continuity.
Relevant to understanding inferred behavioral characteristics of hominids and behavioral capacities shared with other primates.
Developmental biology informs understanding of morphological differences between hominids and pongids and the evolution of distinguishing anatomical features.
Relevant to understanding the geographic distribution and migration patterns of hominid species, from early primates to modern Homo sapiens.
